title: Tax revenue as share of GDP vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Taxes include direct and indirect taxes as well as social contributions. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: GDP per capita is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices.
caption:
Tax revenue as share of GDP vs. GDP per capita

Taxes include direct and indirect taxes as well as social contributions. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between tax revenue as a percentage of GDP and GDP per capita for various countries in 2023. The vertical axis measures tax revenue from 0% to over 40%, while the horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000. Each point represents a country, color-coded by region, with circle size indicating population. A cluster of African nations, including Somalia, DR Congo, Burundi, and Malawi, occupies the bottom-left quadrant, showing GDP per capita below $2,000 and tax revenues generally under 15%. In contrast, European countries like France, Norway, and Germany appear in the upper-right section, with tax revenues exceeding 35% and GDP per capita figures between $50,000 and $60,000. The United States and Switzerland sit distinctly with high GDP per capita near or above $80,000 but lower tax shares around 25% to 28%. Large Asian economies display varied positions; China and India are located in the middle of the chart with GDP per capita between $10,000 and $20,000 and tax revenues near 20%, while Kuwait and Oman appear in the bottom-right with high GDP per capita but tax revenues below 10%. South American countries like Brazil and Argentina show tax revenues near 30% with mid-range GDP per capita. The distribution illustrates that higher GDP per capita does not strictly correlate with higher tax revenue shares, as seen in the separation between high-income, low-tax nations and high-income, high-tax nations.
